speaker: ROMEO
text: Can I go forward when my heart is here?

speaker: ROMEO
text: Turn back, dull earth, and find thy center out.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Romeo, my cousin Romeo, Romeo!

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: He is wise

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And, on my life, hath stol’n him home to bed.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: He ran this way and leapt this orchard wall.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Call, good Mercutio.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Nay, I’ll conjure too.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Romeo! Humors! Madman! Passion! Lover!

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Appear thou in the likeness of a sigh.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Speak but one rhyme and I am satisfied.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Cry but “Ay me,” pronounce but “love” and “dove.”

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Speak to my gossip Venus one fair word,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: One nickname for her purblind son and heir,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Young Abraham Cupid, he that shot so trim

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: When King Cophetua loved the beggar maid.—

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: He heareth not, he stirreth not, he moveth not.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: The ape is dead, and I must conjure him.—

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: I conjure thee by Rosaline’s bright eyes,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: By her high forehead, and her scarlet lip,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: By her fine foot, straight leg, and quivering thigh,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And the demesnes that there adjacent lie,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: That in thy likeness thou appear to us.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: An if he hear thee, thou wilt anger him.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: This cannot anger him. ’Twould anger him

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: To raise a spirit in his mistress’ circle

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Of some strange nature, letting it there stand

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Till she had laid it and conjured it down.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: That were some spite. My invocation

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Is fair and honest. In his mistress’ name,

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: I conjure only but to raise up him.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Come, he hath hid himself among these trees

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: To be consorted with the humorous night.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Blind is his love and best befits the dark.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: If love be blind, love cannot hit the mark.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Now will he sit under a medlar tree

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: And wish his mistress were that kind of fruit

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: As maids call medlars when they laugh alone.—

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: O Romeo, that she were, O, that she were

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: An open-arse, thou a pop’rin pear.

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Romeo, good night. I’ll to my truckle bed;

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: This field-bed is too cold for me to sleep.—

speaker: MERCUTIO
text: Come, shall we go?

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Go, then, for ’tis in vain

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: To seek him here that means not to be found.
